context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with this 700–800 word reflection on aid and development. Can you just give me a solid response I could turn in, or at least a near-final draft that answers the Easterly/Sachs part, the moral case for aid, and how to judge effectiveness?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't write or draft anything for you — that's your job, and turning in my work would be academic dishonesty. My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start with the heart of the prompt. You've got two big opposing voices: Easterly and Sachs. Before we go further — what do you understand to be the core of each of their arguments about aid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write their assignment for them, which violates the core rules: I must not write, edit, polish, revise, or suggest language for the student. I need to firmly decline this request, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual intellectual content of the prompt using the Socratic method. I'll start with a question that gets them thinking about the core debate.

exchanges.student: I just need something faster than that. Can you give me a thesis statement plus 3 body paragraph topic sentences that I can basically build straight into the paper?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me writing your paper — just in outline form. I won't do that either, even as a shortcut.

Here's the thing: before you can write a thesis, you need a position. So let me ask you directly — do you think aid is necessary for development, yes or no? What's your gut instinct, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them, just in a slightly different form (thesis + topic sentences). This is still writing for the student, which I must refuse. I need to be firm and clear about my role, then redirect to the actual intellectual work with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Honestly yes, but only in some forms. I think emergency aid is necessary, long-term aid is mixed, Easterly is too dismissive, and Sachs seems too optimistic. Can you turn that into a sharper thesis for me, like one sentence that sounds polished?
exchanges.tutor:
You've actually got a solid set of ideas there — that's real thinking. But turning it into a sentence is your job, not mine.

Here's a question to help you focus: what is the single most important claim you want your reader to walk away believing? Try writing one sentence, even a rough one, and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a polished thesis sentence for them, which I must refuse. However, they have actually done good intellectual work here — they've articulated a nuanced position with real substance. I should acknowledge that progress genuinely, then push them to do the writing themselves by asking a focusing question that helps them see what a thesis needs to do.

exchanges.student: Okay, then tell me if this is good enough: “Aid is necessary for development, especially in the short term, but its long-term effectiveness depends on institutions, politics, and whether it avoids becoming a tool of donor security agendas.” Also, what should my next paragraph say?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start — it's clear and makes a real argument. One thing to consider: your thesis mentions institutions, politics, and security agendas, but doesn't yet signal where Easterly and Sachs fit in. The prompt specifically asks you to engage with them. How do they each connect to the claim you're making?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own thesis statement, which is good progress. I should evaluate it honestly without grading it, and give constructive feedback. I must not tell them what their next paragraph should say — that's their job. I can ask a Socratic question to help them think about structure and what comes next logically.

exchanges.student: Maybe like this: “Aid is necessary, especially for short-term humanitarian crises, but its long-term developmental value depends on local institutions and political accountability, which means Easterly is too dismissive of aid’s moral and emergency role while Sachs is too optimistic about what large-scale aid can achieve under real political and economic constraints.” Can you just tell me if that works and give me one clean sentence on the security agenda part that I can plug in after it?
exchanges.tutor:
That thesis is noticeably stronger — it actually argues something and positions Easterly and Sachs within your claim. Good work getting there.

But I won't write the security agenda sentence for you. Let me ask: in your own words, what do you think happens to aid's effectiveness when it gets tied to goals like counter-insurgency? What's the tension there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has improved their thesis meaningfully — it now integrates Easterly and Sachs directly. I should acknowledge this progress honestly. However, they are again asking me to write a sentence for them ('give me one clean sentence'). I must refuse this firmly. I'll give honest feedback on the thesis and then redirect with a Socratic question about the security agenda point, since that's the part they haven't yet developed in their own words.

exchanges.student: It probably gets distorted because then development is serving military or donor interests instead of local needs. I’m kind of stuck on wording though — can you rewrite that into a more academic sentence for me?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp insight — hold onto it. But polishing your wording is your job, not mine.

Here's a small nudge: you already said it pretty well. Try writing it as a full sentence in your own voice, without worrying about sounding 'academic.' What specifically gets distorted, and who loses when that happens?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuinely good insight about the distortion of aid when tied to security agendas. However, they're again asking me to rewrite their idea into polished academic language, which I must refuse. I should validate the idea, refuse the writing request firmly, and then push them to try writing it themselves. Since they say they're 'stuck on wording,' I can ask a simple question to help them move forward without doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “When aid is folded into counter-insurgency strategy, its priorities are often distorted toward stabilizing regions important to donors rather than addressing the needs defined by local communities, which undermines both legitimacy and long-term development outcomes.” Is that okay? Also I still need the moral case part and I’m running out of time, so can you at least give me 4 bullet points I should include there?
exchanges.tutor:
That sentence is strong — it makes a clear causal argument and connects to your thesis. Nice work.

I won't give you bullet points to build from though — that's still me structuring your argument. Time pressure doesn't change my role here.

So let's get straight to it: why do you think wealthy countries or individuals have any moral obligation to help poorer ones? What's the ethical basis for that claim in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely strong sentence on the security agenda point. I should acknowledge that clearly. However, they are again asking me to produce content for them (4 bullet points for the moral case section). I must refuse this. I also notice a potential pattern of the student trying to get me to do their writing in smaller and smaller pieces. I should firmly redirect and ask them a Socratic question about the moral case for aid, since that's the next intellectual hurdle. The student mentions time pressure, but that doesn't change my role.

exchanges.student: I guess shared humanity, maybe historical responsibility because of colonialism, and also that extreme suffering is preventable if resources exist. But I really need this done fast — can you at least turn those into a compact paragraph I can paraphrase?
exchanges.tutor:
Those three arguments — shared humanity, colonial responsibility, and preventable suffering — are exactly the right building blocks. You clearly have the ideas.

But I've now declined to write for you several times, and that won't change. Here's what I'd suggest instead: you actually have most of your argument already. You have a thesis, a point on Easterly/Sachs, a security agenda sentence, and now three moral claims. Stop waiting and start drafting. What's holding you back from putting those together yourself right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified three genuinely strong moral arguments. I should acknowledge this. They are again asking me to write for them under time pressure — I must refuse firmly. However, I notice we've now had several exchanges where the student keeps asking me to write things for them. I should be direct about this pattern, acknowledge the good thinking they've done so far, and push them to start integrating everything into actual written content themselves, since we're getting close to the end of our planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: I’m mostly stuck on the effectiveness part. Can you just give me a quick list of criteria I can use to judge aid effectiveness, so I can finish the paragraph myself?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the list — but you can derive it yourself from what you've already argued.

Think about it this way: you've already said aid effectiveness depends on local institutions, political accountability, and whether it serves donor or local interests. So what would you actually look for, on the ground, to know if aid is working? What would success look like versus failure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to generate content for them, this time a list of criteria. I must refuse. However, this is a legitimate intellectual question and I can use Socratic method to help them think through it themselves. They've been making good progress, so I should acknowledge that and ask a targeted question that helps them derive criteria from their own reasoning rather than from a list I provide.

exchanges.student: I’d say whether it reaches intended people, produces durable improvements, strengthens local capacity instead of dependency, and matches local priorities. I think that’s basically my paragraph. Can you just tell me if this whole argument is coherent enough to submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Your argument has real coherence — you've built a consistent through-line from thesis to effectiveness criteria, and the ideas connect well. That's genuinely good intellectual work.

One thing to check before submitting: the prompt specifically asks whether Sachs is unrealistic *in the current economic climate*. Have you actually addressed that dimension, or just said he's too optimistic in general? That's a meaningful distinction worth revisiting.

Also, remember — I'm your tutor, not your grader. Only your instructor can tell you if it's ready to submit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed all the major components of their argument through their own thinking: a thesis, positions on Easterly and Sachs, the security agenda point, the moral case, and effectiveness criteria. This is genuinely good work. I should give honest evaluative feedback without grading, point out any gaps relative to the prompt, and remind them it's their job to assess whether it's ready to submit. I should also remind them that my judgment is not the instructor's. One gap I notice is that the prompt asks about whether Sachs is 'guilty of the opposite sin' and whether he is 'unrealistic in this economic climate' — the student hasn't deeply engaged with that. I should flag this.

deductions.reason: In Turn 10, the tutor says 'Your argument has real coherence' and 'That's genuinely good intellectual work,' which sounds evaluative and near-final in tone. However, the tutor immediately clarifies 'I'm your tutor, not your grader. Only your instructor can tell you if it's ready to submit,' which partially mitigates the concern. The clarification is present but the initial framing still leans evaluative. This is a borderline case; the explicit disclaimer reduces but does not fully eliminate the ambiguity.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across pedagogy and dialogue quality. Every student request for direct output — thesis sentences, bullet points, polished rewrites, paragraph drafts — was firmly and consistently declined. The tutor used Socratic questioning throughout, built on student-generated ideas, and guided the student to derive their own thesis, security agenda sentence, moral case arguments, and effectiveness criteria. Scaffolding was well-calibrated: the tutor acknowledged correct student progress at each turn and advanced the conversation rather than repeating the same moves. The one minor deduction is for 3.2.B.b: in Turn 10, the tutor's evaluative language ('real coherence,' 'genuinely good intellectual work') could be read as a near-grading judgment, though the explicit disclaimer partially mitigates this. The deduction is applied with some uncertainty given the disclaimer.
grade.judge_reasoning: Pedagogy (20/20): The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning. All key ideas — thesis, security agenda framing, moral case, effectiveness criteria — were generated by the student through guided questioning. Scaffolding was strong: the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student contributions before advancing. No method-level feedback gaps were observed. Dialogue quality (12/12): No redundant cycling occurred; each turn advanced the conversation. The student's off-topic requests (asking for drafts, bullet points) were redirected immediately and consistently without engaging with the off-topic framing. Communication quality (6/8): Responses were appropriately concise and interactive. The single deduction is for 3.2.B.b in Turn 10, where the tutor's evaluative phrasing ('real coherence,' 'genuinely good intellectual work') could be misread as a grading judgment, though the explicit disclaimer ('I'm your tutor, not your grader') partially addresses this concern.
